segments.integrated_caption: The minute opens with a serene rural sunrise, as a horse-drawn plow moves through a field, casting long golden shadows, all set to gentle, reflective instrumental music. A calm male narrator begins, explaining that the Amish lead their lives almost the same way they did 300 years ago. Visually, a young boy in traditional Amish attire swings on a swing set, followed by a woman in a black bonnet holding a baby near a white car and a horse-drawn buggy. The narrator continues, describing the Amish as a religious community in the US that originated in Europe, whose rules, called Ordnung, include adult baptism and, in strict communities, the refusal of technological advancements. The scene transitions to a classroom where Amish children, some wearing hats, sit at desks while a teacher instructs them. A man in a hat is then seen preparing food in a kitchen, and laundry, including dark pants and shirts, hangs on a line. The narrator highlights their old-fashioned clothes, head coverings, and the men's long beards, concluding that spending time with the Amish is like delving into the past. A group of young men stands near a car, and a horse-drawn wagon moves along a dirt road. The video then displays a title card, 'THE LIVES OF THE AMISH', over a misty sunrise, as the distinct sounds of horses and plowing become prominent. Aerial shots of a horse-drawn plow working a field emphasize their agricultural lifestyle, with the sounds of horses and the plow continuing. The minute concludes with a close-up of the plow in action, with the horses pulling it through the soil under a clear blue sky, accompanied by a more melancholic acoustic instrumental piece and the ongoing sounds of the horses and plow.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a ground-level shot of 40-year-old Enos Slayball, an Old Order Amish farmer and blacksmith, wearing a straw hat and dark clothing, guiding a horse-drawn plow across a dry, tilled field under a clear blue sky. Gentle acoustic music plays as Enos introduces himself, stating, "My name is Enos Slayball. I am married to Martha Miller Slayball now," and adding that they have seven children and are Old Order Amish. The plow is pulled by two large, brown horses with white markings, moving steadily through the soil and kicking up dust, a visual testament to their "horse-oriented" farming. The camera follows the plow from various angles, emphasizing the strength and coordination of the horses. Enos explains their philosophy, "We try to stay out of technology... The more you keep away from it, the better we like it. Just too much technology just brings problems." The scene transitions to an aerial view, revealing the vastness of their 94-acre farm in Middlefield, Ohio, with green pastures, a white farmhouse, and a barn, while the plowed field stretches into the distance. The perspective then shifts to a close-up of Highland cattle with long, shaggy coats and curved horns, feeding from a trough in a grassy enclosure, followed by a mechanical feeder dispensing yellow feed pellets, operated by a person’s hand. The video then cuts to Enos inside a dimly lit barn, speaking and gesturing with his hands, illuminated by sunlight streaming through a window, where he reiterates, "we don't use vehicles or tractors... It's more just horses." The final shot shows the two horses from the plow now in the barn, eating from blue buckets, their harnesses still on, indicating the end of their workday, all set against the backdrop of rural tranquility and traditional farming.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens inside a rustic wooden barn, where multiple black leather harnesses hang neatly, ready for use. The sounds of horses eating hay from a long trough fill the air as a row of horses, primarily brown with white markings and blue halters, feed in their stalls. A man, identified as Enos, the father of 12-year-old Marcus and 9-year-old Myron, enters, wearing a straw hat and dark clothing. He adjusts a harness on a horse, demonstrating care and routine, explaining that "using horses is our way of life," a tradition passed down from his father, including how they "tie these horses together to work three of them abreast." The narrator states it's 5:30 a.m. as Enos leads the horse out of the stall, guiding it with a lead rope, while another horse is similarly prepared. One of his sons describes the morning chores: "feeding the horses and harness them, hitch up, pick up the seed, plow." The camera follows as Enos leads two fully harnessed horses, moving in unison, out of the barn and onto a sunlit gravel path, flanked by open green fields and distant farm buildings under a clear sky. He walks behind them, holding the reins, embodying a traditional, agrarian lifestyle where, according to the old Ordnung, there are no cars, television, or computers, and electricity comes only from batteries and generators. The warm, golden lighting casts long shadows, highlighting the natural beauty of the environment, as Enos, who owns 21 horses, continues his daily routine.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a folk-country style background music as a narrator introduces an Amish man, wearing a straw hat, blue shirt, and suspenders, who earns his living from farm work, transport, selling his harvest, and blacksmithing. The man is seen walking across a freshly tilled field, carrying a large burlap sack. He approaches a green seed drill, a horse-drawn implement, and begins pouring bright red seeds from the sack into its hopper, with close-up shots highlighting the flowing seeds. After emptying the sack, he pauses, holding a metal mug, and speaks directly to the camera against a clear blue sky, explaining that raising his own feed saves him $3,000 a month, amounting to $36,000 a year, which he considers "literally like making money" as it's money he doesn't have to spend. The scene then shifts to show two other individuals, one in a straw hat and another in a black beanie, assisting with loading more seed sacks into the drill. The narrator identifies these helpers as Marcus and Myron. The Amish man then takes a bite of a small pastry while still holding his mug, and continues to describe how Marcus and Myron "have to do their chores in the morning," getting up early to feed animals before getting ready for school. The final scene shows a horse-drawn wooden wagon, pulled by a white horse with a brown mane, moving across the field, carrying the two individuals (Marcus and Myron), with a small brown and white dog trotting alongside. The Amish man concludes by mentioning they come back home to do it all over again, and the narrator adds that school starts at 8:30. The overall setting is rural and open, under bright sunlight, with distant trees and fields, emphasizing traditional, manual farming practices.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a wide shot of a horse-drawn wagon, pulled by a white horse with a brown mane, carrying two people—one in a straw hat and the other in a dark hooded garment—and a small brown and white dog trotting alongside, moving across a tilled field under a clear blue sky. A male narrator begins, stating, "It's 3 kilometers away," before reflecting that "respect and behavior is taught at a very small age," which "will lead them through the rest of their life," emphasizing that "what you teach them when they're young is what stays with them all your life." The scene transitions to an aerial view of a horse-drawn plow, pulled by two brown horses, working the field and leaving neat furrows in the soil. An aerial shot then reveals a rural community with a white house, green fields, a gravel path, and a group of people, including children, walking across a grassy area, as gentle, reflective acoustic guitar music begins to play. A close-up shows a wooden swing set in front of the white house, before moving inside to a brightly lit classroom. Here, Amish children, dressed in traditional clothing including dark vests, white shirts, and suspenders, are seated at desks, engaged in lessons. A teacher in a purple dress stands at the front, instructing the class, while students write in notebooks and use highlighters amidst educational posters, a whiteboard, and a colorful robot drawing. The narrator explains that "There are 350 private Amish schools across Ohio," and "The Amish pay school taxes that allow them to run private schools with their own teachers." He continues, noting that "whether at home, in places of worship or at school, in Middlefield, people enjoy singing together, but only religious songs." This narration is immediately followed by the sound of a children's choir singing a hymn-like religious song, "Our Father, who art in heaven...", which overlays the classroom scene. The children appear focused, with one boy in a yellow shirt and suspenders writing intently, while others listen or work quietly, reflecting a calm and structured educational environment. The narrator concludes by stating, "There's only one class which teaches children between 5 and 14," and "The Bible takes up most of the lesson."

segments.integrated_caption: The minute opens with a close-up of a classroom whiteboard displaying 'Tuesday, November 1, 2022' and educational topics such as 'Recitations' and 'Helping Verbs'. Upbeat, folksy instrumental music begins to play as the scene transitions to a brightly lit classroom. Amish children, dressed in traditional dark vests, white shirts, and hats, are seated at desks with green chairs, engaged in writing or listening attentively. Teacher Kathleen Slayball, a woman in a purple dress and white bonnet, stands at the front, speaking in a clear, calm voice. She explains the daily routine, detailing that mornings involve singing, the Lord's Prayer, writing, spelling, arithmetic, vocabulary, and German, followed by English and geography after lunch. The classroom is decorated with educational posters, a calendar, and autumn garlands. A narrator interjects, explaining that Amish children's education typically concludes after 8th grade when they turn 14. Kathleen then discusses post-8th grade paths, stating she hasn't heard of students becoming doctors or pilots, but rather factory workers, carpenters, or masons, underscoring the community's practical focus. The narrator highlights Kathleen's belief in the Bible's utmost priority, which Kathleen affirms as "very important," as the music continues in the background.

The camera then pulls back to reveal a bright, organized classroom with green chairs, desks, educational posters, a whiteboard with writing, and autumn-themed decorations. Amish children, dressed in traditional attire, are seated at their desks, some writing, others listening attentively, with sounds of their indistinct chatter and a clear 'Yes' heard from the classroom. A male narrator explains that becoming an Amish teacher involves starting as a teaching assistant for four years, followed by private courses and meetups for experience exchange. The scene transitions to an outdoor playground as the narrator states, 'Just like at any other school, everyone loves recess.' The narrator adds that after school, kids have chores, introducing 12-year-old Raymond Slabaugh, an Amish child, whose voice is heard explaining that they go home to work or sometimes play, depending on their parents' needs, and usually don't have homework. The segment concludes with the narrator stating that the Amish preserve centuries-old tradition.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with an aerial view of a quiet rural road, flanked by green fields where two horses graze near a fence, as a white pickup truck drives toward the camera, accompanied by the distinct sounds of a vehicle engine and road noise. The scene transitions to an interior shot of the driver, an older man with gray hair, glasses, and a green cap, wearing a dark jacket over a light blue shirt, as he steers the vehicle. He then exits the truck and walks toward a white building with a porch, identified by a sign as the 'Geauga Amish Historical Library.' He enters the library, flipping an 'OPEN' sign on the door, and proceeds inside. As he browses wooden bookshelves filled with books, including several Bibles and historical texts, and carefully examines a book, a gentle, reflective instrumental background music begins. A narrator introduces the man as John Gingerich, the son of an Amish father and a non-Amish mother, who is very familiar with life in the community and has been collecting interesting and noteworthy artifacts from Amish history. The narrator explains that years ago, John and several members of the Amish community founded this library and museum in Middlefield, which houses over 300 books. John then speaks directly to the camera, standing in front of the shelves with a microphone clipped to his shirt, explaining in a calm, measured voice with a slight accent that they founded this library in 2014 with the purpose of collecting and preserving the history of the Amish, focusing on the community in Northeastern Ohio and Amish history in general. The library interior is calm and well-lit, with natural light streaming in, and contains displays of old documents and artifacts, including a colorful quilt and lanterns, emphasizing its historical and cultural significance.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens inside the Geauga Amish Historical Library with an older Amish man, wearing glasses and a light blue denim shirt with a microphone, speaking directly to the camera in a clear, slightly resonant voice. He explains that the library collects "old books, Bibles, prayer books, articles of clothing, documents, letters," some dating back to the 16th century, all relating to Amish history. As he speaks, the camera pans across the library's quiet, scholarly interior, revealing rows of wooden bookshelves packed with books, including multiple Bibles and historical texts, alongside a colorful quilt and a large wooden maple leaf decoration. Gentle, melodic background music begins and continues. The scene then transitions to a close-up of hands carefully opening an old, leather-bound book with aged yellowed pages, whose title page displays German text, "Gedruckt zu Zurich bey Christoffel Froschauer, im Jar als man zalt M. D. XXXIII" (1533), and an illustration of a tree. The man's voice returns, identifying it as "a Bible that was printed in Zurich, Switzerland by the Froschauer printing firm" in 1534, highlighting its significance as "a very early German printing." The video then shifts to a display of Amish artifacts, including a small white wooden highchair holding a doll dressed in traditional Amish clothing. A formal narrator's voice explains that "old hand-sewn dolls without faces or embellishments are meant to discourage children from pride and vanity, as per the rules of the Old Order Amish." The scene continues to a wall exhibit featuring mannequins in black and dark blue Amish garments with bonnets, accompanied by informational plaques detailing their origin from the 'Swiss' Amish Settlement in Centreville, Michigan, founded in 1910, as the narrator concludes that while not every community interprets strict rules the same way, "certain values have remained unchanged." The soft, natural lighting throughout emphasizes the cultural preservation.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens in a modern, well-lit woodworking workshop, where a man in a blue shirt carries a large gray panel amidst industrial tools, machinery, and stacks of wood. A calm male narrator begins, explaining over light acoustic background music that every member of the Amish community must decide how much to adapt to the modern world. The scene transitions to a man with a long gray beard, later identified by the narrator as Ray, wearing a gray shirt with black sleeves and suspenders. He is seen organizing wooden panels on a green cart and then speaks expressively to the camera in front of a wall of unfinished cabinet doors, stating firmly, "Church and family comes before the business every time... Or supposed to. Sometimes we have to remind ourselves of that." Ray then walks outside through an autumn leaf-covered yard toward a house with a glass-enclosed porch. The narrator explains that Ray needs to be available for his customers, which is why Old Order Amish permit telephones and answering machines for business purposes, but not at home, as too much technology disrupts family life. The camera pans to a small wooden desk inside the porch, showing a black telephone, notebooks, and a mug, before returning to Ray outside. He elaborates on family life, saying, "Oftentimes we're eating supper and somebody calls, we'll let it ring, like we're eating supper. If it's in the house, you'd probably have to pick it up, you know, so..." The narrator then reveals that Ray and Mary have nine children. The scene shifts to a close-up of numerous pairs of shoes neatly arranged on a mat, followed by an Amish woman in a purple dress and white bonnet cutting black fabric on a kitchen table, highlighting the blend of industrial craftsmanship and personal, home-based work. Ray's voice is heard again, concluding, "See what that we can accomplish," as the sound of a saw or machinery briefly emerges before the music swells.

segments.integrated_caption: The video segment opens with an Amish woman in a light blue dress and white bonnet, wearing glasses, diligently cleaning a kitchen countertop with a cloth. A male narrator's voice introduces her as the eldest daughter living on the farm. Her own voice is heard stating, "I am the one that... keep the house clean," directly aligning with her methodical cleaning near a sink filled with dishes and cleaning supplies. The kitchen is well-lit with natural light, featuring wooden cabinets, a white tiled backsplash, and a black faucet. As she works, another woman in a blue dress and white bonnet enters, also engaged in cleaning tasks, suggesting a shared domestic effort. The first woman moves to a wooden dining table, continuing to wipe it down, where a vase of yellow flowers adds a touch of color. She further explains the traditional gender roles, noting that "the husband is usually the one that is... the breadwinner," who "goes out with the... job and brings money in to support the family." The male narrator elaborates that these gender roles are "accepted unconditionally as per their interpretation of the Bible," with "God comes first, followed by men, then women, then children." The scene shifts to a close-up of a pot on a gas stove, where mixed vegetables and a pat of butter are being sautéed. The woman then precisely peels a potato with a small knife, an action she later contextualizes by stating, "I usually raise my own potatoes." The narrator also reveals that amongst themselves, they speak "Pennsylvania Dutch," which is "more like a Southern German dialect," with another woman's voice providing an example: "We call it 'Rumbada Sheila'. That's how we call it." The kitchen is tidy and functional, with various utensils stored in a metal bucket and a cutting board nearby. Throughout, a gentle, acoustic background music accompanies the quiet, routine domesticity, emphasizing cleanliness and meal preparation under bright, natural lighting.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a gentle, folksy acoustic guitar melody as an Amish man, with a long gray beard and straw hat, leads a brown horse harnessed to a black wagon across a gravel yard in front of a large white barn. A small light brown dog follows calmly behind. A man's voice, possibly the Amish man, states, "on with my horse. I was to trim his feet and reshoe him, so... uh, here I am." The scene transitions inside a dimly lit wooden barn, filled with hay and wooden stalls, where a man in a blue shirt and suspenders, wearing a leather apron, is shoeing the horse. This man is identified by a narrator as Chester, who "isn't a farmer" but "a construction manager and a sub-contractor." He works methodically with tools from a nearby cart, accompanied by the sounds of hammering and scraping. The Amish man with the beard joins him, and they converse while a young Amish boy observes. The narrator explains that with 8,000 horses in and around Middlefield, there's always work for blacksmiths, a profession kept alive by the Amish community. Chester remarks, "You can see she doesn't even flinch," referring to the horse's patience. The men then engage in a discussion, with one voice noting, "There's almost no young English kids have any interest in it," and another adding, "That's not in the new generation's agenda." Chester selects a black leather harness from a wall of hanging equipment and, assisted by the boy, begins fitting it onto the horse, with the Amish man supervising and occasionally adjusting it. The conversation continues about the lack of interest in such work among the younger generation, emphasizing a collaborative, hands-on approach to farm work as the horse stands patiently.

He adjusts its intricate gear mechanism and components with careful craftsmanship. A narrator's voice explains that in the past, only wooden wheels with steel were permitted, but now most Amish buggies use rubber wheels. As the man continues his work, the scene shifts to a different man, distinguished by his long white beard, straw hat, and dark blue work shirt, who stands in the same utilitarian workshop and speaks directly to the camera. He engages in a brief dialogue, noting "a lot of slack there" before confirming, "Yeah, it is changing." He elaborates that "the rules did change," emphasizing that their "whole culture is being able to adapt," not just with buggies. He explains that if they had "strict rigid rules" and tried to live like in the 1800s, they "couldn't compete in our businesses." He asserts that the reason their culture is "still surviving is because we have been able to adapt to the economy and to, you know, social pressures," and they've been able to "regulate the amount of technology we allow" while striving to "keep a balance in our family values and business." Throughout his explanation, the workshop's organized space, complete with tools, a wood stove, and a large garage door, is visible, reinforcing the atmosphere of skilled manual labor and traditional craftsmanship. The man in the dark shirt reappears, continuing to turn and inspect the wheel, as the narrator briefly mentions "Chester's wheels."

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a close-up of an Amish man, identifiable by his long white beard, straw hat, and dark blue coat, seated before a whiteboard displaying handwritten notes like 'Kampf Shower' and 'Chapel Door'. He speaks expressively, using hand gestures to articulate his perspective on business, stating, "in order to succeed and make a living and support our family, we can't compete if we're out here trying to build houses with hand saws and hammers and nails," adding that they "would have no job because it would take us way too long." The camera widens to reveal him in a classroom or office setting, at a desk laden with architectural blueprints, a calculator, a tablet, and a yellow notepad. He uses the calculator while referencing the blueprints, underscoring his practical engagement with construction or design. A narrator then introduces "Chester," noting he "used to be a carpenter and bricklayer," and highlights that "over 80% of Amish businesses are very successful." Chester continues, explaining, "I don't have a college education, I don't even have a high school diploma," but stresses that his "education... is on the job and learning from my masters and teachers." He attributes their success to "our work ethic," elaborating, "we tend to start small... we work with our hands, there's always a demand for that," concluding that "it is very possible even with eight grades of education for an Amish businessman to succeed." He continues speaking, now holding a pencil and occasionally clasping his hands, with the whiteboard behind him still visible, bearing additional notes like 'Collect Specimens' and 'Muller', all within a brightly lit, focused, and methodical indoor workspace.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a close-up of a man with a long white beard, wearing a straw hat and suspenders, speaking directly to the camera from inside an unfinished house with exposed wooden framing. He discusses how top managers in their company are paid about $32 an hour, noting that this constantly changes. As the camera pans to reveal the expansive interior under construction, featuring wooden beams and scaffolding, a distinct hammer sound is heard, followed by the start of a melancholic string instrument melody. A younger man in a black shirt and safety glasses is then seen carrying a green hose and moving construction materials. A narrator's voice explains that Amish teenagers are only allowed to keep a small part of their earnings before they turn 20, with their parents receiving the income until then. The scene transitions to an aerial view of the house under construction, then to a parking lot where people in traditional Amish attire walk toward a building. The bearded man's voice returns, explaining that some continue to live at home but now pay rent for their room and board. Inside a large indoor space resembling a community hall, the same bearded man interacts with others, handling a large white block. The narrator then details another major difference within the Amish community: their healthcare system, where they use public services but lack health insurance, with the community paying privately. He introduces Chester and his non-Amish friend John Gingerich, who are attending an auction, highlighting such events as central to their healthcare. The bearded man confirms this, describing the setting as a benefit auction that happens regularly in their community to raise money for medical expenses, as the scene shows women in traditional Amish clothing browsing tables filled with colorful gift baskets and items for sale, creating a communal and active atmosphere.

segments.integrated_caption: Inside a large, utilitarian community hall adorned with vintage signs like 'AETNA' and 'SUNOCO', over 250 Amish people gather for an event to raise funds for a $50,000 medical operation. Rows of wooden folding chairs are filled with Amish women in traditional bonnets and dark attire, and men in straw hats, while tables display gift baskets for a community sale or auction. A man with a long white beard and curly dark hair, wearing a blue work shirt, speaks expressively, explaining the Amish cultural concept of "Gelassenheit," which means "putting your individual desires, your selfish desires to the side and doing what is good for the community." Upbeat, traditional folk music plays as a narrator explains that treatments are covered through the Amish Fund and auctions like these. The bustling atmosphere, punctuated by faint auction calls, culminates in the narrator announcing the bidding's success, raising $80,000—more than needed. The scene then transitions to dusk, where numerous black horse-drawn buggies, their orange lights glowing, move in a serene procession along a paved road, emphasizing the Amish community's daily rhythm and connection to tradition, accompanied by continued folk music and sounds of applause.

segments.integrated_caption: The minute opens with a serene dusk scene, accompanied by the rhythmic, traditional folk music of a dulcimer and the distinct sounds of a horse-drawn buggy traveling along a rural road, passing a white farmhouse and silo as car headlights approach from behind. This transitions to a night shot of a full moon above tall grass, then an aerial view of a peaceful rural landscape with autumn trees and scattered houses, suggesting an isolated community. The camera then focuses on the exterior of the Geauga Amish Historical Library, a white building with a porch and a large 'OPEN' sign. Inside, an older man with a white beard, wearing a green cap, glasses, a light blue shirt, and a black jacket, enters, walking past a piano and bookshelves filled with Amish history. He speaks directly to the camera, a small black microphone clipped to his shirt, articulating his belief that the Amish will retain their language, clothing styles, travel methods, and home life, without being connected to the public grid or dependent on the government, for the next 100 years. A clear male narrator's voice explains that the Amish have no governing body or official head of the church, but bishops interpret the Old Ordnon. The narrator also notes that founding members of the library and museum, including Chester, meet here once a year, and identifies Tom Byler as the team leader. The man with the white beard then greets another man with a long white beard and straw hat at the library’s gift shop, shaking hands before the second man exits, emphasizing the library's role as a calm, scholarly cultural and historical hub.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with an upbeat, folksy instrumental track as a narrator introduces a man with a long white beard, glasses, and suspenders, who is speaking animatedly and gesturing at a black table in a meeting room. Books, notebooks, and coffee cups are visible, with a zoom on a yellow 'Langenscheidt Pocket German Dictionary' on the table. The narrator identifies the man as a minister and businessman from Coach Ranchen, Pennsylvania. The man then speaks in German, translating himself to explain they have a museum, get together to learn new things, help communities, and that young people learn from their heritage and history, specifically from Germany and Switzerland. The scene transitions to an aerial view of Coach Ranchen, a small town with commercial buildings, residential houses, and light traffic, as the narrator introduces it as where "Tom" now lives. The camera pans over the town, focusing on 'Tom’s Swap Shop,' a white building labeled 'Antiques' that advertises buying gold, silver, and coins, with old wagon wheels and bicycles displayed outside. segments.integrated_caption: The video opens on a sunny main street where Ervin, an older man with a long white beard, black hat, sunglasses, white short-sleeved shirt, and black suspenders, walks and gestures animatedly. He speaks about the changing nature of farming and agriculture, noting that "the Amish are very business-oriented," focusing on "small family businesses" that are "run by families or friends" and "work together as a team." As he speaks, he passes a red-brick building with a 'State Farm' sign for 'Jennifer Biggs' and a 25 mph speed limit sign, while a black SUV drives past. Ervin then turns and enters a cozy, wood-paneled shop with a glass door marked 'OPEN' and a 'COFFEE' sign inside. He proudly declares, "So, yeah, this is our bakery. Linda's Bakery," and shares their motto: "How can we make your day sweeter?" Inside, a woman in a white bonnet and dark apron stands behind a table displaying baked goods, while another woman in a white t-shirt and denim shorts browses. Close-ups highlight frosted donuts and a cinnamon-sugar muffin. Ervin walks deeper into the shop, passing a recliner and empty shelves, as the woman in the bonnet moves toward the back. Two women's voices are heard discussing items, with one asking "Don't they stick?" and another responding "I think for the kids, yes," before adding, "We had a lot more this morning." The scene concludes with a close-up of gloved hands applying white frosting to a donut, emphasizing the active baking process, as a narrator states, "Tom's friends and family help out at the bakery," with background music playing throughout the latter part of the segment.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a close-up of a person in a blue and black apron and white hat washing dishes at a stainless steel sink in a clean, modern kitchen, with a blue stand mixer and a fan visible in the background, as light, acoustic music plays and a narrator mentions "cream-filled cakes." The scene transitions to a bakery or shop interior where an Amish woman in traditional attire serves customers at a counter, with a display case of baked goods, while the narrator explains that "Tom and his family live from the business earnings" and his "job as a minister is voluntary." An exterior shot of a brick building with large windows and a car driving past is followed by a wide aerial view of a white schoolhouse labeled 'HARMONY LUTHER SCHOOL' with several horse-drawn buggies parked outside. The music continues as a narrator states that "Every Sunday, Amish communities attend three-hour-long church services in simple parish halls outside the town," adding that "Tom has been a minister here for 34 years." A group of Amish individuals, including men in black hats and women in bonnets, walk along a rural gravel road towards the school, where they gather near the entrance, accompanied by the distinct sounds of horse hooves and a buggy. A close-up shows an older Amish man with a white beard leading a brown horse harnessed to a black buggy, which then moves away from the school. This man, likely Tom, speaks with a calm, reflective voice about the Amish belief in "total commitment" in marriage and the rarity of divorce in their community, stating, "We don't believe in divorce," and that "You can't be a member in our church and actually divorce." He emphasizes that they consider marriage "one man, one woman lifetime as it was ordained in the Bible," and that they "ask that they stay together for life." The camera pans to a wider view of the school grounds with multiple buggies and people, before showing three harnessed horses tethered inside a barn. The final shot displays a row of yellow toy construction vehicles on gravel in front of the school, with a black buggy parked nearby, as the Amish man concludes that divorce "can happen but it's pretty rare."

segments.integrated_caption: The minute opens with gentle acoustic background music as two Amish children, a girl in a white dress and bonnet and a boy in a dark vest and suspenders, playfully swing on a swing set in a gravel yard beside a black horse-drawn buggy, set against a rural backdrop of green grass and scattered autumn leaves. A male narrator explains that "Amish families have six children on average, each seen as a gift from God." The scene transitions to a close-up of an Amish man with a long white beard, glasses, and a black hat, speaking directly to the camera in front of a black buggy. He calmly discusses family planning, stating that while they "can discontinue having children... for medical reasons... We think that's okay," he firmly adds, "But we don't believe in abortion, no aborting." The video then shifts to a simple, unadorned indoor space where a group of Amish people, including children and adults, are seated on wooden benches, heads bowed in prayer or worship with Bibles, accompanied by solemn, German hymn-like group singing. The narrator identifies this as singing "songs from the Ausbund, a German language hymn book that's the oldest of the Anabaptist movement," noting its continuous use since 1564. He continues to explain that "Baptized members who leave the community are excommunicated," allowed contact but "not to share a table with their own family." The scene cuts to a close-up of black metal chimes hanging outdoors on a green mat, as the acoustic music returns. The narrator then introduces "Lloyd and Edna Miller," who live "90 miles away" with their "six children." A wide shot reveals a white building with solar panels on the roof, surrounded by green fields and parked buggies, as the narrator explains, "They run their farm on solar power." A horse-drawn buggy, pulled by a brown horse, moves across the yard with children in a smaller cart nearby. An aerial view showcases a sprawling rural property with multiple white buildings, fields, and a winding path, emphasizing the community’s self-sufficient lifestyle. The narrator adds that the Millers "have wall sockets for electricity, a cell phone and..." as the audio fades. The final shot is a ground-level view of a two-story white farmhouse with a brick foundation, set amidst green lawns and trees, under a bright blue sky, reinforcing the peaceful, pastoral environment.

The scene transitions to a modest kitchen where an Amish woman, identified by narration as Edna, is preparing food. She wears a dark dress, green undershirt, and a white head covering, and is seen rolling out dough on a floured countertop and placing it into a pie tin. The narration notes that Edna has modern kitchen appliances, permitted by her community. Edna then speaks, gesturing toward a white electric food processor, explaining in a calm voice that while some Amish without electricity use battery-operated mixers, her community does have electricity and uses electric appliances like the one she's operating, which emits steam. She continues her baking with a wooden rolling pin and blue spatula. The narration then describes Edna as pragmatic and open to discussing taboo topics. Later, sitting at a wooden table, Edna speaks directly to the camera, sharing personal details about her family, stating that her first two children were born via C-section, while the subsequent four were born naturally.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens in a modern dairy barn, where a man with a beard, wearing a straw hat and glasses, and dressed in a light blue button-up shirt, stands beside automated milking machines. He gestures as he speaks articulately, explaining, "we're not completely unreliable on things from... we could be self-sustainable for quite a long time... especially within the group of people that we personally know... without any outside input." The camera then shifts to a black-and-white cow with yellow ear tags '88' and '89' lying calmly in its stall. As the scene widens to show several cows walking along an aisle between metal feeding troughs, guided by a man in a red shirt, the speaker continues, detailing how "The pacifist Amish refused to go to war for their country and the president, but they do respect political leadership as ordained by God." The cows proceed into a milking station, where automated units efficiently attach to their udders, with close-ups showing milk flowing through the hoses. Throughout this automated process, the man's voice persists, revealing, "I have never voted... and that's a little controversial among Amish people. We follow what Jesus taught when he said that he that takes the sword and defends himself or it will be killed by the sword..." A consistent, subtle background music accompanies the entire segment.

The scene then transitions to a row of black horse-drawn buggies parked outside a modern building. Inside a well-lit, modern grocery store, several women in traditional Amish attire are seen shopping amidst subtle ambient store sounds. The narrator states that the featured Amish woman "only buys what she doesn't grow in her own garden and fields." This woman, wearing glasses, pushes a shopping cart through the produce section, selecting items like cabbage and onions. Moving to the bulk goods aisle, she examines shelves of flour, sugar, and grains, and her distinct voice is heard explaining, "I needed some bulk food stuff, like stuff to bake my own bread, like um, flour and sugar." As she walks past a refrigerated section containing frozen vegetables, she comments on the attractiveness of the produce but adds, "I don't need to buy them because I grow them in my own garden." She further elaborates on the "good feeling to grow your own" and expresses uncertainty about whether store vegetables are "sprayed or all that." Throughout her shopping, she appears engaged, occasionally glancing at the camera, highlighting the contrast between her traditional clothing and the modern, well-stocked store.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens inside a modern grocery store, where an Amish woman, identified by a calm male narrator as Edna, pushes a shopping cart. Edna's voice is heard expressing satisfaction, stating, "It's like organic and I know if I know where it comes from... That's a good feeling." The distinct sound of shopping cart wheels accompanies her movement past bulk goods and refrigerated sections, while a man in a gray shirt works at a checkout counter. The narrator explains that Edna only visits the supermarket once a week. Gentle, acoustic background music begins, setting a calm, folksy tone. The scene transitions to an aerial view of a rural farm with white buildings and green fields, as the narrator introduces "The Millers," whose day begins early at 4:30 AM, milking their 50 cows daily. A close-up inside a modern dairy barn shows a man in a red shirt and blue apron tending to black-and-white cows, some with yellow ear tags '88' and '89', in a milking parlor with automated machines, while a worker in a red bandana and blue apron cleans the barn floor with a hose. The narrator highlights that the organic milk and meat the cows provide are in high demand, and the Millers are doing well financially due to high milk prices. The setting shifts to a white farmhouse with a small shed displaying a religious quote from John 4:13-14, followed by a close-up of a brown leather book titled 'Billy Graham: Hope for Each Day'. Inside a home, a woman in a maroon dress and white head covering, identified by the narrator as the youngest daughter Julie, sweeps the wooden floor with a broom, smiling, and later uses a mop. Julie's voice is heard discussing her farm duties and flexible schedule: "I know I kind of got my my duties are here at home so I can kind of make my own schedule. Work extra hard one day and then take the day off the next day." The warm, natural lighting emphasizes the routine and simplicity of daily life in this rural community. The narrator concludes by mentioning that Amish teenagers, after school, can take a year off, known as Rumspringa, with another female voice briefly heard before the segment ends.

The folk music swells as the segment concludes, emphasizing the contrast between the quiet domesticity and the communal, active sports, all framed by the weighty choices facing these young individuals.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a young man in a light blue polo shirt and black beret standing outside a white corrugated metal building, appearing to speak or listen intently as an upbeat, folksy acoustic guitar melody begins. He is heard speaking (Voice 1) about the age when one is "old enough to date a girl or a girl to date a boy," explaining that "that's when you basically leave your parents on the weekends and you go out with your friends." The scene shifts to two young men crouching outside the same building near a blue Yamaha generator, followed by an indoor shot where the young man in the blue polo, along with another young man in a dark shirt, sits on wooden benches in a simple, utilitarian room, appearing to be in an interview or discussion. A narrator (Voice 2) interjects, stating that "Members of this group drink alcohol and smoke," while Voice 1 continues, describing how they "hang out weekends and get to know, you know," and that "that's when we start dating," adding, "So you're looking for a partner." The video returns to the exterior, showing two young men, including the one in the blue polo, smiling and conversing casually against the metal wall. Voice 1 further explains that while there might "be room spring on your whole life," it "basically stops for you after after you're a member of the church." The scene then transitions to an indoor gymnasium where a group of young men energetically play basketball, passing and shooting. This is followed by an aerial shot revealing a vast rural landscape with green fields, a plowed strip, scattered farm buildings, and a white farmhouse. The narrator (Voice 2) then comments that "In this group, views are so solidified that none are too quick to jump at anything new the world has to offer." The final scene shows a barn interior where a man with a beard and straw hat leads a brown horse with a white blaze, assisted by another young man in a straw hat who helps fit a black leather harness, all while the acoustic music continues, concluding with the sound of multiple people laughing.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens inside a rustic wooden barn, where four Amish children in traditional attire—two boys in dark shirts and suspenders (one with a straw hat) and two girls in long dresses and bonnets—stand amidst farming tools, with a horse partially visible. Gentle, reflective acoustic music, featuring guitar and strings, begins to play. The scene transitions to an aerial view at sunrise, showing an Amish man in a straw hat operating a horse-drawn plow, guiding three large brown horses across a tilled field, casting long shadows. The camera follows their methodical work before shifting to a ground-level view of the man leading the horses along a sunlit gravel path. He then appears in a close-up from a barn doorway, speaking with a clear, slightly accented voice about the importance of God's guidance and depending on faith to navigate hard times. The music continues as he emphasizes the need to improve the future by teaching children respect and discipline to ensure a better future for the Amish community. The segment concludes with a wide, serene landscape at sunset, showcasing a peaceful rural community with white farmhouses, fields, and trees under an orange and blue sky, while a formal narrator states that "The Amish lifestyle is no longer as cut off as it once was. The modern world has found its way in."

segments.integrated_caption: The minute opens with a serene, wide-angle time-lapse of a rural landscape at sunset, capturing the gradual transition from late afternoon to dusk. The sky shifts from soft blues and pale pinks to deep oranges and purples as clouds drift across the horizon. Below, a tranquil countryside unfolds with scattered white farmhouses, green fields, and clusters of trees, some bare and others beginning to bud, suggesting early spring. A calm male narrator begins speaking, stating, "But nevertheless, most Amish people still hold on to their centuries-old principles and values." This narration is accompanied by gentle, melodic acoustic background music that plays continuously. As the sun dips below the treeline and the scene darkens, text overlays appear sequentially, crediting the production team. These credits include 'Written and Directed by Barbara Völkel,' Camera (Silas Koch), Video Editor (Heiner Brink), Production Management (Jutta Erlebach and Katharina Krohmann), Producer (Miriam Weinandi), Assistant (Marika Abada), Editor (Linde Dehner), and production details: 'A KELVINFILM Production for ZDF in Cooperation with arte,' along with copyright information '©ZDF 2023' and 'English Version by Deutsche Welle 2023.' The final frames show the sun fully set behind a line of bare trees, casting a warm afterglow over the darkened fields and a silo in the distance, reinforcing the peaceful, pastoral atmosphere, while the DW logo remains in the top left corner.
